The Helmholtz energy of the rubber is written as a sum of two components: (i) elastic energy due to the deformation of the crosslinked network and (ii) an entropy of mixing of the un-crosslinked molecules with the sites for these molecules such that
div.formula: 𝐹(𝜀 𝑖𝑗 , 𝑐, 𝒯) ≡ [ 𝜆 2 (𝜀 𝑘𝑘 𝑒𝑙 ) 2 + 𝐺 erf ( 𝜂 𝜂 0 ) 𝜀 𝑖𝑗 𝑒𝑙 𝜀 𝑖𝑗 𝑒𝑙 ] + 𝑅𝒯𝑐 𝑅 [𝜃 ln 𝜃 + (1 -𝜃) ln(1 -𝜃)],(4.3)
div.p: where 𝑅 is the universal gas constant and 𝒯 the absolute temperature. In (4.3) 𝜆 and 𝐺 are the first and second Lame parameters, respectively such that the first term represents the elastic energy while the second term is the contribution from the entropy of mixing. In the elastic energy there is a term associated with the strain triaxiality
div.formula: 𝜂 ≡ √(2/3)𝜀 𝑖𝑗 𝑑 𝜀 𝑖𝑗 𝑑 |𝜀 𝑘𝑘 | ,(4.4)
div.p: where the deviatoric strain 𝜀 𝑖𝑗 𝑑 ≡ 𝜀 𝑖𝑗 -𝛿 𝑖𝑗 𝜀 𝑘𝑘 /3 with 𝛿 𝑖𝑗 the Kronecker delta. This term was introduced for the following reason. Recall from Supplementary S3 that the rubber is an isotropic material and thus in the absence of imposed deviatoric strains we require the rubber to have a purely hydrostatic response. To ensure that this remains the case independent of the choice of 𝑒 [𝑝] we introduce the strain triaxiality term in (4.3) such that the contribution to the elastic energy due to the shear modulus vanishes for vanishing total deviatoric strains. In (4.3), 𝜂 0 sets the triaxiality range over which we apply these additional considerations to ensure isotropy come into play and a choice 𝜂 0 ≪ 1 implies that this additional term only serves as a regularising parameter. Given the Helmholtz free-energy 𝐹, the material stress and the chemical potential of the un-crosslinked species follow as
and
respectively.

Now consider a rubber specimen occupying a domain Ω subjected to surface tractions 𝑇 𝑖 = 𝑇 𝑖 0 on a portion 𝑆 𝑇 and displacements 𝑢 𝑖 = 𝑢 𝑖 0 on a portion 𝑆 𝑈 of the boundary 𝑆 of the domain Ω. The potential energy of this system then
div.formula: Π(𝑢 𝑖 , 𝑐) = ∫ 𝐹𝑑Ω Ω -∫ 𝑇 𝑖 0 𝑢 𝑖 𝑑𝑆 𝑆 𝑇 ,(4.7)
div.p: and at equilibrium we then require that the total variation 𝑑Π = 0. Then using (4.5) and (4.6) it follows that under isothermal conditions
div.formula: ∫ 𝜎 𝑖𝑗 𝛿𝜀 𝑖𝑗 𝑑Ω Ω + ∫ 𝜇𝛿𝑐𝑑Ω Ω = ∫ 𝑇 𝑖 0 𝛿𝑢 𝑖 𝑑𝑆 𝑆 𝑇 ,(4.8)
div.p: where the strains are related to displacement via 𝜀 𝑖𝑗 ≡ 0.5(𝑢 𝑖,𝑗 + 𝑢 𝑗,𝑖 ). Since the variation in displacement and concentration are arbitrary it follows that
div.formula: ∫ 𝜎 𝑖𝑗 𝛿𝜀 𝑖𝑗 𝑑Ω Ω = ∫ 𝑇 𝑖 0 𝛿𝑢 𝑖 𝑑𝑆 𝑆 𝑇 ,(4.9)
div.p: and

The total amount of the un-crosslinked phase in the specimen is a constant (i.e., the uncrosslinked phase does not leave or enter the specimen). Therefore, ∫ 𝛿𝑐𝑑Ω = 0 Ω . It then follows from (4.10) that at equilibrium the chemical potential of the un-crosslinked phase throughout the specimen is a constant such that 𝜇 = 𝜇 0 with 𝜇 0 set by the conservation law ∫ 𝑐𝑑Ω = 𝑐 𝑅 𝜃 0 Ω Ω . Similarly, applying the divergence theorem to (4.9) we recover the usual stress equilibrium relation 𝜎 𝑖𝑗,𝑗 = 0 with boundary conditions 𝑇 𝑖 0 = 𝜎 𝑖𝑗 𝑚 𝑗 on 𝑆 𝑇 where 𝑚 𝑗 is the outward normal to 𝑆 𝑇 .

To complete the formulation, we now need to specify a constitutive relation between 𝑒 [𝑝] and the concentration 𝑐 of the un-crosslinked phase. Physical considerations impose important restrictions on the relation between 𝑒 [𝑝] and 𝑐 as follows. Recall that the measurements suggest that the overall volume of the specimen is conserved such that ∫ 𝜀 𝑘𝑘 𝑑Ω = 0 Ω irrespective of the nature of the loading. Given that the elastic strains are strongly dependent on the nature of the loading it is therefore reasonable to assume that 𝜀 𝑘𝑘 𝑒𝑙 contributes negligibly to 𝜀 𝑘𝑘 (i.e., the rubber is elastically nearly incompressible implying that 𝐺/𝜆 → 0) with the volumetric strains predominantly set by the swelling strains 𝑒 [𝑝] . Then the constraint ∫ 𝜀 𝑘𝑘 𝑑Ω = 0 where 𝛿𝑐 is an arbitrary variation. But the conservation of un-crosslinked phase implies that ∫ 𝛿𝑐 𝑑Ω = 0 Ω and thus (4.12) can only hold for arbitrary 𝛿𝑐 if 𝜕𝑓/𝜕𝑐 is a constant, i.e. 𝜀 𝑗𝑗 𝑠 is a linear function of 𝑐. We thus choose the form 𝑓(𝑐) = 𝜀 𝑗𝑗 𝑠 = 𝑘(𝜃 0 -𝜃), where 𝑘 is a material constant so that (4.11) is always satisfied.

Given the above constraint on the form of the volumetric swelling strain we propose that the principal swelling strains written in terms of 𝜃 ̅ ≡ 𝜃/𝜃 0 take the form 𝑒 [1] = 𝑘𝜃 0 𝛼 1 (1 -𝜃 ̅ ); 𝑒 [2] = 𝑘𝜃 0 𝛼 2 (1 -𝜃 ̅ ); 𝑒 [3] = 𝑘𝜃 0 (1 -𝛼 1 -𝛼 2 )(1 -𝜃 ̅ ), (4.13) where the material parameters (𝛼 1 , 𝛼 2 ) govern the extent of the deviatoric swelling strains. For example, with the choice 𝛼 1 = 𝛼 2 = 1/3 the swelling is purely volumetric (as typically assumed for hydrogels). This completes the specification of the constitutive model for rubber in the equilibrium setting.

div.head: S4.3: Extension to the non-equilibrium setting
div.p: We now wish to determine the kinetic laws for the case where loading is sufficiently fast that the specimen may not be under equilibrium conditions at every stage of the loading. Recall that the rate of change of potential energy Π ̇ of the system satisfies the inequality Π ̇≤ 0 so that (4.17)

For the choice 𝑁 = 1 with 𝑀 the linear mobility of the mobile species. Given Φ D we can then re-obtain the flux relation (4.16) by following Onsager [32][33] (also see [34]), viz, define an augmented potential

div.formula: ∫ 𝜎 𝑖𝑗 𝛿𝜀i 𝑗 𝑑Ω Ω = ∫ 𝑇 𝑖 0 𝛿𝑢̇𝑖𝑑𝑆 𝑆 𝑇 ,(4.24)

div.head: S4.4: Analysis of the 4-point bend test
div.p: In a 4-point bend test (Fig. 2a), the central section of length 𝑠 1 between the two inner rollers is under pure bending. We define a co-ordinate system 𝑥 𝑖 ′ with the origin at the centre of the beam and use the above constitutive model to analyse a beam of thickness ℎ, under plane stress conditions, in pure bending along the 𝑥 1 ′ direction (Fig. S19a). The total strain-rate fields are independent of 𝑥 1 ′ and given by

in terms of the imposed curvature rate 𝜅. The problem specification is completed with the plane stress boundary condition 𝜎 22 = 0 and the traction conditions 𝑇 𝑖 = 0 on 𝑥 3 ′ = ±ℎ/2. Under these conditions the principal directions coincide with the global co-ordinate system 𝑥 𝑖 ′ such that the principal stresses are Σ 1 = 𝜎 11 , Σ 2 = 𝜎 22 and Σ 3 = 𝜎 33 . Then the relation between principal stresses Σ 𝑖 and corresponding principal strains ℰ [𝑖] (defined by 𝜀 𝑘𝑙 𝑛 𝑙 [𝑖] = ℰ [𝑖] 𝑛 𝑘 [𝑖] ) is given by Σ 𝑖 = 𝜆[ℰ [1] + ℰ [2] + ℰ [3] -𝑘𝜃 0 (1 -𝜃
and chemical potential is 𝜇 = 𝜆𝑘 𝑐 𝑅 [ℰ [1] + ℰ [2] + ℰ [3] -𝑘𝜃 0 (1 -𝜃 ̅ )] -2𝐺erf ( 𝜂 𝜂 0 ) [(ℰ [1] -𝑒 [1] ) 𝜕𝑒 [1] 𝜕𝑐 + (ℰ [2] -𝑒 [2] ) 𝜕𝑒 [2] 𝜕𝑐 + (ℰ [3] -𝑒 [3] ) 𝜕𝑒 [3] 𝜕𝑐 ] + 𝑅𝒯 ln 𝜃 1 -𝜃 .

Recall that the principal directions of the swelling strains are chosen to coincide with the principal directions of total strains so as to minimise 𝜀 𝑖𝑗 𝑒𝑙 𝜀 𝑖𝑗 𝑒𝑙 . Here we choose 𝛼 1 = -𝛼 2 = -1 and therefore the choice 𝑞 𝑘 [𝑖] = 𝑛 𝑘 [𝑖] minimises 𝜀 𝑖𝑗 𝑒𝑙 𝜀 𝑖𝑗 𝑒𝑙 . This implies 𝑒 [1] = -𝑘𝜃 0 (1 -𝜃 ̅ ); 𝑒 [2] = 𝑒 [3] = 𝑘𝜃 0 (1 -𝜃 ̅ ).

With the swelling strains now known in terms of 𝜃 the expressions for the stresses and chemical potential can be further simplified. The equilibrium condition 𝜎 𝑖𝑗,𝑗 = 0 combined with the traction boundary condition 𝑇 3 = 0 on 𝑥 3 ′ = ±ℎ/2 implies that Σ 3 = 0. Then from (4.26) it follows that ℰ [2] = ℰ [3] such that (4.26) and (4.27) reduce to
and
div.formula: 𝜇𝑐 𝑅 = 𝜆𝑘[𝜅𝑥 3 ′ + 2ℰ [3] -𝑘𝜃 0 (1 -𝜃 ̅ )] -2𝐺𝑘erf ( 𝜂 𝜂 0 ) [{𝜅𝑥 3 ′ + 𝑘𝜃 0 (1 -𝜃 ̅ )}
div.p: respectively. The condition that Σ 2 = Σ 3 = 0 can then be used to determine ℰ [3] from the relation 𝜆[𝜅𝑥 3 ′ + 2ℰ [3] -𝑘𝜃 0 (1 -𝜃 ̅ )] + 2𝐺erf ( 𝜂 𝜂 0 ) [{ℰ [3]

Finally, we need a condition to determine 𝜃. The evolution of 𝜃 depends on the kinetics of transport of the un-crosslinked species and we first consider the limiting case of loading with 𝜅̇→ 0. In this limit the chemical potential is given by the equilibrium condition 𝜇 = 𝜇 0 : this constant chemical potential is determined by writing 𝜃 in terms of 𝜇 0 from (4.30) and then calculating 𝜇 0 from the conservation constraint
Now consider the case of a finite 𝜅̇ where the concentration of the un-crosslinked species may not be in equilibrium. In this case, the flux of the un-crosslinked species is given by
which combined with the conservation law gives the overall differential equation for concentration of the un-crosslinked species as

with initial conditions 𝜃 = 𝜃 0 ∀ 𝑥 3 ′ at time 𝑡 = 0 and boundary conditions 𝑗 3 = 0 at 𝑥 3 ′ = ±ℎ/2. Thus, we need to solve equations (4.31) and (4.34) together with the boundary conditions to obtain solutions for 𝜃(𝑥 3 ′ ) and ℰ [3] (𝑥 3 ′ ).

div.head: S4.5: Material parameters and comparison with measurements
div.p: The model requires the specification of the following 5 parameters (in addition to the choice we have made of 𝛼 1 = -𝛼 2 = -1) in the equilibrium limit of an infinitesimal loading rate. These are the shear modulus 𝐺, the Poisson ratio3 𝜈, the constant 𝑅𝒯𝑐 𝑅 governing the entropy of mixing, the swelling strain constant 𝑘 and the occupancy 𝜃 0 of the mobile phase in the undeformed configuration. These parameters were determined as follows. The Poisson ratio was selected to be 𝜈 = 0.45 so that the rubber is nearly elastically incompressible and then 𝐺 chosen to match the moment versus curvature relations. The parameters 𝑅𝒯𝑐 𝑅 , 𝑘 and 𝜃 0 were chosen together to obtain the measured strain (ℰ [3] and volumetric strain 𝜀 𝑘𝑘 ) distributions within the beam. Now consider the parameters governing the transport of the un-crosslinked polymer. In general, two additional parameters are required, viz. 𝑁 and 𝑀𝑗 0 (𝑁-1) to fully characterise the non-linear transport. However, the measurements show that the response of the Silicone rubber is nearly rate independent over the 3 decades of loading rates investigated here. This implies that equilibrium is attained much faster than the loading rates we have considered in this study. Within the framework presented here this implies that the un-crosslinked phase is an approximately rate independent shear thinning fluid with 𝑁, 𝑗 0 /𝑀 → 0. In this limit equilibrium with vanishing spatial gradients of 𝜇 is attained instantaneously, i.e., the problem reduces to the equilibrium problem discussed in Section S4.2. All the parameters inferred for Silicone rubber are listed Table S3 and comparisons between the simulations and measurements are included in Figs. 2 and3 of the main text.

div.head: S4.6: Simulation of the tracer mobility measurements
div.p: The tracer radiographs (Fig. 3d) were the key measurements that provided evidence of the rapid transport of the un-crosslinked species. Those measurements (Fig. 3e) shows that the spatial distribution of mobile phase is independent of the loading rate. It is thus critical to simulate the motion of these tracers in the limit 𝑁 → 0. In this limit time is no longer an independent parameter and thus we need to re-cast the transport equations of Section S4.3.
Mass conservation of the un-crosslinked species is written as
where time 𝑡 = 𝜅/𝜅̇ and 𝑗3 ≡ 𝑗 3 /𝜅. Here 𝜕𝑐/𝜕𝜅 is known from the equilibrium solution procedure of Section S4.4, i.e. given by (4.25) -(4.32). The normalised flux is therefore

since 𝑗̂3(𝑥 3 ′ = -ℎ/2) = 0. We shall now use this flux to determine the motion of the tracers. The tracer particles were assumed to be attached to a fraction 𝜒 0 of the un-crosslinked molecules and this concentration is approximately constant over the region -ℎ/2 ≤ 𝑥 3 ′ ≤ ℎ 𝑇 0 where 𝑥 3 ′ = ℎ 𝑇 0 is initial position of the tracer front as seen in Fig. S17a. To avoid a step function, we assumed a smooth distribution of the initial tracer concentration given by

where the constant ℎ 𝜀 ≪ ℎ is a regularising parameter that smoothens the step function of the initial concentration of the tracers; see Fig. S19b for a plot of (4.37) for the choice ℎ 𝑇 0 = -0.6 mm and selected values of ℎ 𝜀 /ℎ (in Fig. S19b we use the experimental parameters of the beam thickness and ℎ 𝑇 0 from Fig. 3d). Recall that the tracers are attached to the un-crosslinked molecules and thus their motion follows directly from the flux of the un-crosslinked species. In particular, given the current tracer concentration 𝑐 𝑇 (𝑥 3 ′ ), the flux of the tracers is 𝑗 𝑇 = 𝑗 3 𝑐 𝑇 /𝑐 and the evolution of the tracer concentration follows from the conservation equation 𝑐̇𝑇 = -𝜕𝑗 𝑇 /𝜕𝑥 We then re-cast this equation using the transformations 𝑡 = 𝜅/𝜅̇ and 𝑗̂3 ≡ 𝑗 3 /𝜅̇ as

This equation, with 𝑗̂3 given by (4.36) is solved with the initial condition (4.37) along with boundary conditions 𝑗 𝑇 = 0 on 𝑥 3 ′ = ±ℎ/2 to determine the motion of the front of the tracer particles.

Solution of this transport equation requires the specification of three additional parameters, viz. ℎ 𝜀 , ℎ 𝑇 0 and 𝜒 0 . The initial position of the tracer front ℎ 𝑇 0 follows directly from the radiographs (Fig. 3d) while ℎ 𝜀 is a numerical parameter that we choose to be as small as possible but still allowing the numerical calculations to converge. Our tests demonstrated that ℎ 𝜀 = 0.01ℎ suffices. Finally, we need to choose 𝜒 0 . This parameter is not precisely known but given that approximately 5% by wt. ZnI 2 was dissolved into the catalyst we take 𝜒 0 = 0.05: we shall subsequently show that the choice of 𝜒 0 over the range 0.005 ≤ 𝜒 0 ≤ 0.2 has a minimal effect on the predictions.

Predictions of the spatio-temporal evolution of 𝑐 𝑇 with the measured value of ℎ 𝑇 0 = -0.6 mm (and choice 𝜒 0 = 0.05) are shown in Fig. S20a for the experimentally applied loading protocol, viz. 𝜅ḣ = 0.12 s -1 over 0 ≤ 𝑡 ≤ 4 s and 𝜅ḣ = 0 for 𝑡 > 4 s. The tracer concentration evolves such that the tracer concentration reduces near the compressive side of the beam and increases near the front of the tracers. We define the position 𝑥 𝑓 of the "front" as the value of 𝑥 3 ′ where 𝑐 𝑇 is a maximum (with 𝑥 𝑓 = ℎ 𝑇 0 at 𝑡 = 0). The predicted temporal variation of 𝑥 𝑓 for this case is included in Fig. S20b. Comparisons between the measurements and these predictions of the tracer front are included in Fig. 3e and show excellent agreement. Calculations for predictions of the spatio-temporal evolution of 𝑐 𝑇 for two additional values of 𝜒 0 are included in Figs. S21a and S21b. These results are nearly indistinguishable (and also nearly identical to the predictions in Fig. S20a). This demonstrates that the value of 𝜒 0 has a negligible influence on the predictions. Table S2: The optimal parameters for the hyperelastic modules as deduced by the FEMU analysis along with the corresponding values of the optimised cost functions 𝒞 𝑢 , 𝒞 𝑃 and 𝒞. This data is shown for properties inferred from the tensile tests on the (a) 𝐻/𝐷 = 1 and (b) 𝐻/𝐷 = 2 specimens with 𝐷 = 28 mm.

figure.head: Figure 1 :
figure.figDesc: Figure 1: Globally incompressible deformation of Silicone rubber accompanied by local volume changes. (a) The loading setup, with in-situ XCT imaging. The computed grayscale tomograph of a 600 μm cube within a cylindrical Silicone rubber specimen obtained from FETC illustrates the inherent speckle within the material. (b) Cylindrical Silicone rubber specimens of varying aspect ratios 𝐻/𝐷 glued to platens and subjected to uniaxial tension. (c) XCT image of the 𝐻/𝐷 = 1 specimen deformed to 𝜀 ∞ = 0.21 along with the evolution of the spatial distribution of volumetric strain Δ𝑉/𝑉 0 on internal planes with increasing strain 𝜀 ∞ . (d)The nominal tensile stress 𝜎 ∞ versus strain 𝜀 ∞ curves for specimens with aspect ratios in the range 1 ≤ 𝐻/𝐷 ≤ 10 and (e) comparison of the volumetric strains at 𝜀 ∞ = 0.21 for the range of specimen aspect ratios investigated here. In (c) and (e) the volumetric strains are plotted on the deformed configuration, and we include for each case the volumetric deformation 〈𝑉/𝑉 0 〉 averaged over the whole specimen.

figure.head: Figure 2 :
figure.figDesc: Figure 2: Bending of the Silicone rubber provides insights into deformation mechanisms. (a) Sketch of the 4-point bending apparatus with leading dimensions labelled (dimensions listed in Supplementary TableS1). (b) Comparison of the measured and predicted moment 𝑀 versus normalised curvature 𝜅ℎ response for selected loading rates 𝜅ḣ. (c) Measurements and predictions of the moment relaxation response for step loading under 4-point bending. The inset shows a sketch of the loading history with the imposed curvature increasing linearly up to time 𝑡 = 𝑡 𝑅 such that the final curvature is 𝜅 ∞ ≡ 𝜅ṫ 𝑅 . (d) Spatial distribution of volumetric strain Δ𝑉/𝑉 0 within the beam bent to 𝜅 ∞ ℎ = 0.24 and (e) corresponding distributions of selected components of the Green-Lagrange strain 𝐸 𝑖𝑗 .

figure.head: Figure 3 :
figure.figDesc: Figure 3: Evidence of the presence of a mobile phase within Silicone rubber and measurements of its mobility. (a) X-ray tomographic reconstruction of a composite Silicone rubber beam with dissolved ZnI 2 in the lower half serving as an X-ray tracer. The sketch shows the setup of the fast radiography 4-point bend setup with the collimation slit. The definition of the local co-ordinate system 𝑥 3 ′ fixed to the neutral axis of the beam is also indicated. (b) Xray tomographic reconstructions of the composite beam subjected a normalised curvature 𝜅ℎ = ±0.24. The two curvatures correspond to bending of the beam with the tracers on tensile and compression side, respectively. (c) Predictions and measurements of the variation of the volumetric strain Δ𝑉/𝑉 0 as well as predictions of the normalised concentration 𝜃 ̅ of the mobile phase across the thickness of the beam subjected to 𝜅ℎ = 0.24. (d) Montage of radiographs showing a central section of the composite beam subjected to a step loading 4-point bend experiment with 𝜅ḣ = 0.12 s -1 and 𝑡 𝑅 = 4 s (i.e. 𝜅 ∞ ℎ = 0.48). (e) Comparison between measurements and predictions of the tracer front position 𝑋 𝑓 as a function of normalised time 𝑡/𝑡 𝑅 . The two cases with 𝜅ḣ = 0.12 s -1 (𝜅 ∞ ℎ = 0.48 and 0.24) correspond to 𝑡 𝑅 = 4 s and 2 s. Error bars associated with the measurements are included (Supplementary S3). In (d & e) time 𝑡 = 0 corresponds to the instant that loading commenced.

(b) The measured applied load 𝑃 versus displacement 𝑈 of central roller for the 3-point bending of geometrically identical Silicone rubber, Nylon6 and HDPE beams. In each case we include the volumetric deformation 〈𝑉/𝑉 0 〉 averaged over the whole specimen.

𝑑Ω = 𝑐 𝑅 𝜃 0 Ω Ω as the total number of molecules of the un-crosslinked phase is conserved

The decrease in potential energy drives a dissipation rate 𝑑 ̇ due to the transport of the mobile phase with a flux 𝑗 𝑖 , i.e. Π ̇= -∫ 𝑑 ̇𝑑Ω Ω . We define a dissipation potential Φ D defined such that𝑑 ̇= 𝑗 𝑖 𝜕Φ D 𝜕𝑗 𝑖 . (4.15)To motivate the functional form of Φ D we extend the usual Darcy flow mechanism of flow of a Newtonian fluid through a porous medium to the case where the fluid is shear thinning. Analogous to Darcy flow, the gradient of the chemical potential drives the flux of the mobile phase. The flux in case of a shear thinning fluid is then assumed to be given in terms of a reference flux 𝑗 0 and a mobility 𝑀 by 𝑗 𝑖 = -𝑗 0 𝑁 ≤ 1 is the shear thinning exponent of the mobile phase. The relation (4.16) reduces to a Newtonian fluid for 𝑁 = 1 while in the limit 𝑁 & 𝑗 0 /𝑀 → 0 the flux 𝑗 𝑖 → ∞ for a finite spatial gradient of the chemical potential, i.e., we expect equilibrium corresponding vanishing gradients of the chemical potential to be established nearly instantaneously.To establish the form of Φ D we first relate 𝜇 and 𝑗 𝑖 to the dissipation rate. Taking the rate form of the potential energy (4.7) we obtain Π ̇(𝑢̇𝑖, 𝑐) = ∫ 𝜎 𝑖𝑗 𝜀i 𝑗 𝑑Ω Ω

figure.head: Figure S18 :
figure.label: S18
figure.figDesc: Figure S18: Sketch of the envisioned microstructure of the Silicone rubber comprising a network of the crosslinked polymeric chains with the un-crosslinked non-spherical molecules fully or partially filling the pores. (a) The undeformed or reference configuration subjected to a spatial gradient of loading similar to bending and (b) the deformed configuration where the un-crosslinked molecules have migrated from the region of compressive stresses to the region of tensile stresses. This migration is accompanied by dilation of the pores on the compressive side (and vice versa) as well as changes in the shapes of the pores associated with the nematic ordering (alignment) of the non-spherical molecules. To better illustrate the deformation of the crosslinked network, the undeformed network is simplified as a square grid in (a) and shown as white lines in (b) along with the deformed crosslinked network. The dilation due to reduction in attractive forces between the un-crosslinked molecules and the network is illustrated by the pale background while the darkened background denotes the increased attractive forces causing the contraction of the pores.

figure.figDesc: Figure S21: Predictions corresponding to Fig. S20 but with different choices of 𝜒 0 . Spatiotemporal evolution of 𝑐 𝑇 /𝑐 𝑅 with choices (a) 𝜒 0 = 0.005 and (b) 𝜒 0 = 0.2. The definition of the tracer front position 𝑥 𝑓 is indicated.

𝑈 we neglect the contribution from material point 𝑘 at loading stage 𝑗. 𝜆 2 , 𝜆 3 , are the 3 principal stretches. It is then useful to define invariants of the isochoric deformation as 𝐼 ̅ 1 ≡ 𝐽 -2/3 𝐼 1 and 𝐼 ̅ 2 ≡ 𝐽 -4/3 𝐼 2 . The strain energy density function 𝑊 is then written in terms of (𝐽, 𝐼 1 ̅ , 𝐼 ̅ 2 ). The first Piola Kirchhoff stress 𝑃 𝑖𝑗 (or nominal stress) and then determined from 𝑊 by

note.p: Modular arithmetic is the system of arithmetic for integers where the numbers "wrap around" when reaching a certain value, called modulus. For example, in a 12-hour clock if it is 7:00 now in 8 hours it will be
3:00 since the clocks "wraps around" every 12 hours. Thus, in (4.2) if 𝑝 = 3 & ℓ = 2; 𝑝 + ℓ (mod 3) = 2.
𝜈 is related to the first Lame parameter via 𝜆 ≡ 2𝐺𝜈/(1 -2𝜈).

div.p: Supplementary References [1] Hubbell, J.H., Seltzer, S.M., (2004) Table S1: Dimensions and materials of all specimens investigated here. The definitions of the geometric parameters are in Fig. 1 for the cylindrical specimens and in Figs. 2 and4 for the 4point and 3-point bending specimens, respectively. The table also provides the radiography parameters for all test configurations considered in this study. All tests using the W target were conducted at a power of 30 W while the Mo target tests used a power of 10 W.

div.head: . X-Ray Mass Attenuation Coefficients, NIST Standard
Material parameter
div.p: Table S3: The material parameters for the thermodynamically consistent model for Silicone rubber. These parameters are used for all the predictions shown in Figs. 2 and3.

div.head: Supplementary Movies
div.p: Movie S1: A walkthrough into the volume of the 𝐻/𝐷 = 1 Silicone rubber specimen (Fig. 1e) subjected to a nominal strain 𝜀 ∞ = 0.21 showing the spatial variation of Δ𝑉/𝑉 0 .
Movie S2: A walkthrough into the volume of the 4-point bend specimen Silicone rubber specimen (Fig. 2d) showing the spatial variation of Δ𝑉/𝑉 0 at nominal strain 𝜅ℎ = 0.24 .

div.head: Movie S3:
div.p: A walkthrough into the volume of the bent composite beams (Fig. 3b) to show transport of the ZnI 2 tracers when the tracers were present on the (a) on the compressive side and (b) on the tensile side.

Movie S4: Video stitched together from the radiographs of Fig. 3d of the composite beam subjected to a step loading 4-point bend experiment with 𝜅ḣ = 0.12 s -1 and 𝑡 𝑅 = 4 s (i.e., maximum 𝜅ℎ = 0.48). The total time of the experiment is 𝑡 = 10 s and shows there is no motion of the tracer beyond 𝑡 𝑅 = 4 s. The video was constructed from radiographs with an interframe time of 0.5 s.

div.head: Movie S5:
div.p: A walkthrough into the volume of the 3-point bend specimen of the commercial Nylon6 (Fig. 4c) showing the spatial variation of Δ𝑉/𝑉 0 at a displacement 𝑈 = 4 mm.
